speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is August 8, 1996. Interview of survivor Yehoshua Trigor, born T-R-I-G-U-B-O-V-A-S. The interviewer is Susan Douglass. City/state-- Deal, New Jersey. Country, USA. The language is in English. My name is Susan Douglass. S-U-S-A-N D-O-U-G-L-A-S-S. Today's date is August 8, 1996. I will be interviewing Yehoshua Trigor in Deal, New Jersey, USA. The-- the interview will be in English. Please state your name and spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Yehoshua Trigor. Y-E-H-O-S-H-U-A T-R-I-G-O-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please state your name at birth and spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name was Avsejus Trigubovas. T-R-I-G-U-B-O-V-A-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: What--

speaker: interviewee
text: Trigubovas.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born the 1st of July, 1921.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm now 75.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Mazeikiai, northwestern Lithuania.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please spell the town.

speaker: interviewee
text: M-A-Z-E-I-K-I-A-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Please say and spell the names of your parents, your father first.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was Jakov Leib Trigubov.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please spell that. Or Trigubovas. J-A-K-O-V. Leib. L-E-I-B. T-R-I-G-U-B-O-V-A-S. And your mother's full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Esther nee Feinberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please spell that.

speaker: interviewee
text: E-S-T-H-E-R nee Feinberg. F-E-I-N-B-E-R-G.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many other children besides you were in the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister, older sister. The name was --==

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please spell that for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: G-I-T-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was she born?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was born in the same town.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What did your father do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a businessman. We owned a store, a sale of furs, imported hats, and uniforms.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of uniforms?

speaker: interviewee
text: Army, [NON-ENGLISH]-- that means the-- like police and similar organizations.

speaker: interviewer
text: What role did your mother play in the business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she was a full partner and full-- part of the business. She sold the goods. She was a saleswoman. And she also was playing host to very important customers, bring them, sometimes, tea and cookies and confiture, reserved for very important clients.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any particular important client that stands out in your mind?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There was a General Pelechavicius, who was instrumental in overthrowing the democratically elected leftist government in Lithuania in 1926. And his mother, who was a connoisseur of fur, good furs, she used to buy. And whenever she came to the store, she always had the honor of having the special tea and cookies.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell his name, the name of this general you've just mentioned?

speaker: interviewee
text: P-E-L-E-C-H-A-V-I-C-I-U-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, your father's business, did this involve any traveling?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where would he travel?

speaker: interviewee
text: He would travel mainly to [NON-ENGLISH], to Memel, called in Lithuanian, Klaipeda. And before the Nazis came to power, also to [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever travel with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very seldom, to Memel and to [NON-ENGLISH]. I didn't travel to East Prussia. I was too small.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were some of your father's interests besides his business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Many Zionist activities. He was one of the founders of the Hebrew [INAUDIBLE] Gymnasium Talbot. He was on the board of Talbot. And he was a worker for the [NON-ENGLISH], which is the precursor of-- of the UIA.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you--

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- oh, United Israel Appeal. He also was interested in the local synagogue. There was only one synagogue in our town, as this was a new town, comparatively. And when the reader, the Torah reader was away, or was not well, then my father used to read the Torah.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Now, you say this was a new town. What do you mean by that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean that the country, to most towns in Lithuania, 150 years ago, our town was just a village on a farm by a person called Mazeika And when the Russian government, the czar, brought the [NON-ENGLISH] railroad line to [NON-ENGLISH] via Mazeikiai, then the railroad brought a great deal of life, especially since they also built a road-- a railroad from Mazeikiai to Riga. And then, of course, being the center of a railroad, of-- of course the station, and the-- the-- the people that-- that worked around the railroad, during the Russian times, there was also a railroad school and also a huge workshops to build and repair locomotives and railroad cars.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which czar are we talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: The last czar.

speaker: interviewer
text: And And that would be?

speaker: interviewee
text: That would be, I guess-- what was his name? The last Romanoff, anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: The last Romanoff. So, the Russian government.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father ever consider having the family immigrate to Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My father very much wanted to-- to do so, and actually encouraged my late sister, Gita, to go on [NON-ENGLISH] and prepare herself in Lithuania on a farm, and then go to Palestine, which she did in 19-- I believe-- 34.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about plans for you, your mother, and your father to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father very much wanted to-- to do so. But it was not so easy. There was a economic crisis in Lithuania, as it was in most of Europe in the '30s. And the money was very tight. You sell-- you sold many-- mostly on credit. Otherwise, people couldn't afford to buy-- in installments. And it was very difficult to collect quickly and to capitalize and-- [INAUDIBLE] and go away. There was also question in the '30s that the British have imposed restrictions on Jewish immigration into Palestine. And you could only go as a either capitalist, for which you needed at least 1,000 sterling, which was at that time a very substantial sum, or else as a student at-- at a school of higher learning. And I was too young for that. So this was our, I would say, double jeopardy. And we could not actually move.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the date-- the period of time when your sister left?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember vaguely, but there were many friends came, of course, to the railroad station to say goodbye to her. And then she joined, in [NON-ENGLISH], some other people who went. And then they took a train to Germany, to, I believe, to Italy, where they boarded a-- a ship and landed in Palestine. She was a member of a kibbutz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which kibbutz? Do you recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: A kibbutz of Hashomer Hatzair.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was there for a few years until few years she got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Let's go back to your life in town. What do you remember most about your home as you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a very happy Jewish home, centered around Israel and the Jewish politics and the Jewish press. The language at home was either Hebrew or Yiddish. And sometimes, also German. And when the parents didn't want us to understand, they spoke to each other in Russian. Not in German, because German was similar to Yiddish. And we would-- we would have understood.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a happy home. I studied before that in a Hebrew primary and high school for part of the time. Then my father decided to-- since I could not go through the full course of eight years in our town in Hebrew, because we only had the [INAUDIBLE] gymnasium, he decided that I should transfer to the-- to the Mazeikiai state gymnasium, which was in Lithuanian and had a slightly different-- slightly different program. So I had to prepare myself and I had the exams. I pass the exams. And then, of course, I studied in Lithuanian high school.

speaker: interviewee
text: At the same time, I continued to have Hebrew Bible and Talmud classes every day, by a special teacher that my father imported from [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH]. And the [INAUDIBLE], the-- my rabbi was called Rabbi [NON-ENGLISH] [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH] [NON-ENGLISH]. And like in the Bible, I was Yehoshua. I was the pupil, the disciple of Moses. So-- and he was very proud of it. I was his first student. And of course, other people-- after my father brought this teacher from another town-- I mean, let him-- they also enjoyed studying with the same teacher.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. What was your favorite subject at school, as you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Literature.

speaker: interviewer
text: Literature. Literature.

speaker: interviewee
text: Literature. We had-- I was very lucky to have, in the state high school, a very bright teacher. He was actually a former pilot at the Lithuanian Air Force. But because of problem-- vision problems, he had to use glasses-- at that time, they wouldn't have pilots with glasses.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he went to Grenoble in France for a second degree, for a-- in-- in literature. And when he came back, he was a teacher of Lithuanian language and-- and literature. And literature meant [INAUDIBLE] literature. So at the beginning of every year, he used to give us a list of 65 or 70 books that we had to read during the year. And we were examined on this. And of course, he used to take, let's say, from the French literature, Moliere, from English literature, Shakespeare; from the German literature, of course, Goethe, Schiller, Heine, [NON-ENGLISH], Uhland. So we got a very well-rounded-- also from the Polish literature, [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], or [NON-ENGLISH]. So-- and of course, from the Russian literature, that was Gogol and Dostoyevsky. So I would say that I was very lucky to have such a teacher and such a high school where we received an excellent education. Mm-hmm. While you were going through your schooling, before the university, did you engage in sports?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did engage in sports. I played soccer and also engaged in track and field-- 100 and 200 meters and long jump. Every schoolboy and schoolgirl in Lithuania, in order to graduate, had to achieve a certain minimum in about 10 different fields of sports. And you had to have either a bronze or a silver sign at your lapel for graduating. Unless you were physically unable and certified so, you had to have this kind of achievement in sports.

speaker: interviewer
text: In this public gymnasium, what was the size of your class?

speaker: interviewee
text: The class was between 40 and 50. Sometimes, like, two, three boys more, or sometimes girls. But it was more or less half and half.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. As you were going through this public gymnasium, or as you were growing up in your town, did you ever experience any anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not. But I was told-- and that's hearsay, of course-- that when I was small, that there were some stones thrown at some Jewish homes. I did not experience it. And I must say that in all the years that I spent-- six years-- in the Lithuanian state gymnasium, I did not experience any discrimination or anti-Semitism at all. There was very strong discipline. And I'm sure there must have been, among the students, some who had such anti-Jewish feelings. But that they didn't have an opportunity to express themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: In this public gymnasium, were there are other Jewish students like yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And the son of the local Dr. [NON-ENGLISH] and myself, we were the first two Jewish students. Afterwards, there were about 50 all in all. That means about 10% of the-- 10% of the student body was Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did you start at the public gymnasium?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '33. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] until '39.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: '39. I graduated and then I left. At that time, big changes took part in Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hitler attacked Poland. Poland fell apart.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. We'll come back to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that particular time in a moment. I want to go back to some aspects of your life in your hometown. What was your favorite Jewish holiday?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our favorite Jewish holidays were, I believe, Hanukkah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was that your favorite?

speaker: interviewee
text: You must realize that those who grew up in a Jewish national atmosphere and longed for the restoration of Jewish independent life, at the age of 10, 11, read books like [NON-ENGLISH] and others based on that period. So for them, the holiday of Hanukkah was the expression of Jewish revolt, of the rule of right and not might, and few against many. So it just fitted us perfectly well. And Hanukkah was a very big holiday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. While you were growing up and going to school, what role did you play in Zionist activities?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was a member of Betar. And I participated in all the Betar activities. We had camps and lectures. We were interested in Palestine. I was also a member of Maccabi.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which is?

speaker: interviewee
text: A sports organization. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we also had a group that was interested in travel. And we used to travel very often, inside and outside Lithuania. As peoples of the state gymnasium, we had very inexpensive railroad tickets. And we used to go to, as an example, in 1938, I went to the European basketball championships held at Riga. In '39, I went to the basketball games held in-- in [NON-ENGLISH], in Lithuania, in [NON-ENGLISH]. Now, we also traveled to hear Jabotinsky, as an example. And the other leaders sometimes invited by some friends. And of course, [INAUDIBLE] participated in camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: You, therefore, you heard Jabotinsky speak. What do you remember of his comments?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember mainly something that if the Jews would have listened to-- and I don't know if everybody was able to-- would have saved us, probably, a great deal of blood in Second World War. Because he simply said to Jewish people, abandon the galut-- that means the diaspora. Otherwise, the diaspora will devour you. And he said that the war is imminent and Jewish life is in danger, and that whoever can save himself should save himself. And some young people, especially from countries closer to the Mediterranean, were able to do it. We were in a more difficult situation. And I was too small. My father was, you know, had a family, a-- a wife, and a business, and so on. And most of us also didn't know when it will happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Jews postponed and postponed, until it was probably too late. Then Poland fell apart and the Russians came and and so on, and-- All right. Let's just-- we're just going to complete some comments about your education. After you finished the gymnasium, you decided to go to Vilna University. Was it hard or easy to get into the university?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not difficult in-- the difficulty for Jews was only if they wanted to study medicine or agriculture. Or law. But I wanted to study economics and government.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was no problem in that. There was also no problem in chemistry. Now, you have to understand that in Lithuania, 80% of the doctors were Jews. And 84% of the lawyers were Jews. And the nationalist government, egged on by maybe also anti-Semitic propaganda to some extent, wanted to Lithuanize the professionals more. I mean, they wanted to see more Lithuanian doctors and more Lithuanian lawyers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. How would you describe the government in 1939 when you-- the Lithuanian government-- when you got to university?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1939, it was already a little different. It was not the same government that was since 1926. Because after having suffered a ultimatum from Poland to drop demands for Vilna-- Vilnius-- Vilna, in Polish-- and having lost in March '39, Memel, to the Germans, to the Nazis, the Lithuanian government had to be expanded to include also the left and the right. So at that time, it was already a government which was headed, I believe, by General Cernius. And it had some people from the right and from the left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell the name of the general who headed the government in '39?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe-- I'm not so sure, but I believe it was Cernius. C-E-R-N-I-U-S. And also, after him was the-- another [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] no. I don't-- I don't--

speaker: interviewer
text: Fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember the other name.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's fine. OK. When you moved to Vilna to attend the university, where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived, in the beginning, on-- in a hotel on Szopena street. S-Z-O-P-E-N-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed there a couple of months until I found, with a friend of mine, an apartment on [NON-ENGLISH]. That means [? Valenska ?] Street.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. And how long did you live at that apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived there through the-- all the time until the Germans game.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, while you were going to Vilna, what did you major in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Economics. And the-- they didn't have foreign affairs. But they had what they called, more or less, politics. So-- but of course, this changed when-- after the Russians got hold of Lithuania.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then-- then, of course, politics became involved with Marxism and Leninism.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. While you were going to university, did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning, I worked as a-- an-- with a official interpreter of the Lithuanians district court of Vilna. I translated from Lithuanian-- actually, from Polish and from Russian into Lithuanian. That's what I did. After a few months, I landed a job in Vilna, in Vilnius, in a suburb called [NON-ENGLISH]-- I mean, that's where they sent me-- to work in the Lithuanian passport department. It belonged to the ministry of the interior. And it was around [INAUDIBLE] the municipality of Vilnius.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get that job?

speaker: interviewee
text: Simply by applying. Simply by applying. I was the only Jew. All my friends were Lithuanian students, boys and-- men and women, who all did the same thing. And this gave us flexibility, because we worked only till 2 o'clock, I believe, in the afternoon, or till 3:00. And we had all the afternoon and the evening free for studies.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did this affect your attending classes at the-- this job affect your attending classes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it didn't affect very much. Sometimes, there was some conflict. But it could be resolved with the-- with the professor or with the tutor. There was no-- no big problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was the passport-- why did you feel the passport job was a better job?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I could use-- I could use, there, Lithuanian, Polish that I learned in the meantime, Russian, and Yiddish. You must realize that some of the refugees that came from Poland, like [INAUDIBLE] yeshivas, the [? Mir ?] Yeshiva, as an example, and other yeshivas, [NON-ENGLISH] Yeshiva, came-- they run away from Poland to Lithuania. And they had to go through the passport offices. So sometimes, the yeshiva boys didn't speak even Polish, only spoke Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at that time, the manager said, Mr. Trigubovas, he says, this is a client for you. And I spoke to them in Yiddish and I found out exactly where they came from. And we used to grant, according to the law and to the situation of every person, each person was to give him either a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] refugee card-- that's like-- like a [INAUDIBLE] pass-- or a sort of identity card, which did not prove his being a citizen or not. Or a-- a Lithuanian passport. All right. Besides the yeshiva students that you dealt with from Poland, were there other groups from Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were many Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. Were there any non-Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sure there were any non-Jews. Yes. Yes, of course. I dealt with many non-Jews that were Poles who ran away. They didn't want to be interned by the Russians or by the Germans. And Lithuania was a [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] neutral country.
